text: This video demonstrates our technique for a W ileal neobladder

After the cystectomy and pelvic lymphadenectomy, the surgeon determines the position for urethral anastomosis approximately thirty-five centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve. The bowel is inspected to identify the optimal segment for reaching the urethra. The chosen position is marked with a long stay suture, which is exteriorized through a 5mm laparoscopic port. A short stay suture is placed distally for orientation. The specimen is extracted through a mini laparotomy, and the bowel is exteriorized using the long stay suture.

Sixty centimeters of ileum is used to create the neobladder. The surgeon performs a hand-sewn small bowel anastomosis extracorporeally, creating an enterotomy 15 centimeters from the distal end of the neobladder segment for urethral anastomosis. The bowel is reintroduced into the peritoneal cavity, and the robot is redocked. A continuous anastomosis is sewn between the neobladder and the urethra using a double-armed threostratafix suture. The first limb of the neobladder is detubularized and sutured together, starting 3 centimeters from the distal end and continuing to the midpoint of the ileal segment. Stay sutures are placed between the two walls using ovicral on a CT needle, followed by a continuous suture line with a three-oh stratafix.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to retract vicryl stay sutures while performing a continuous suture line. An additional fifteen centimeters of the ileal segment is detubularized, and the second limb of the posterior neobladder wall is sutured together. The detubularization and sewing of the walls are performed limb by limb to prevent disorientation, using interrupted vicryl stay sutures for bowel opposition and orientation, followed by a continuous stratafix suture line.

The surgeon detubularises the final fifteen centimetres of the ileal segment, leaving a three centimetre ascending limb for future ureteric anastomosis. The posterior wall of the neobladder is completed and sutured. The neobladder is folded, and stay sutures are placed to form the anterior wall, with the apex of the posterior wall folded down to the midpoint of the anterior wall and secured with a stay suture.

The surgeon closes the anterior wall of the neobladder using two layers of continuous 3-0 Stratafix sutures, while the posterior wall is closed with a single layer. The proximity of the small bowel mesentery minimizes leakage from the posterior wall, whereas the anterior wall requires a double layer closure to prevent leaks.

The surgeon anastomoses the ureters to the ascending limb of the neobladder. The left ureter is delivered between the small bowel mesentery and the mesentery of the sigmoid colon, spatulated widely, and sewn with a three-oh stratific suture. A seven French bander stent is passed into the ureter and through the limb of the neobladder, exiting through the small bowel mesentery. The anastomosis is completed, and the stent ends are withdrawn through a five millimeter port in the left iliac fossa.
